title: Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years
subtitle: Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"
caption:
Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years

Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"

This stacked bar chart displays survey responses from eleven countries regarding the anticipated impact of artificial intelligence over the next two decades. The data is segmented into five categories: "Mostly help" (blue), "Neither" (green), "No response" (purple), "No opinion or don't know" (tan), and "Mostly harm" (maroon). Japan and China show the highest levels of optimism, with 61% of respondents in both nations selecting "Mostly help." Germany follows closely at 60%, while Italy stands at 50%. In contrast, Pakistan records the lowest share of positive expectations at 19%, with the majority of its respondents, 52%, anticipating that AI will "Mostly harm" society. Indonesia also shows a high level of concern, with 33% expecting harm and 44% expressing no opinion. The United States and India both report 35% and 36% respectively for the "Mostly harm" category, while their "Mostly help" figures are 35% and 33%. France and the United Kingdom fall in the middle range, with 40% and 47% expecting help, and 36% and 29% expecting harm. The "No opinion or don't know" category varies significantly, ranging from 5% in Japan and France to 44% in Indonesia. The survey included a global total of 120,000–130,000 people aged 15+, with most countries represented by a nationally representative sample of around 1,000 people.
